one_liner: A 32-year-old Ethiopian immigrant presents with 3 weeks of progressive headache, low-grade fevers, confusion, and a cranial nerve VI palsy, with CSF showing lymphocytic pleocytosis, markedly low glucose, and elevated protein.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient emigrated from Ethiopia 2 years ago. He has been experiencing 3 weeks of progressively worsening headache (initially mild, now severe and constant), intermittent fevers up to 38.5°C, night sweats, fatigue, and anorexia with 4 kg weight loss. Over the past week, his wife notes he has become increasingly confused, with difficulty following conversations and excessive somnolence. Two days ago, he developed double vision. He has no history of TB treatment or known TB contacts but did not undergo TB screening upon immigration. He worked in a refugee camp in Ethiopia before emigrating. He denies cough, hemoptysis, or chest pain. No history of HIV testing. No immunosuppressive medication use.
stage2.pmh: None known. No prior TB screening.
stage2.family_history: Mother died of 'lung disease' in Ethiopia (details unknown).
stage2.social_history: Ethiopian immigrant (2 years in US). Works as a parking garage attendant. Lives with wife and two children in a small apartment. Non-smoker. No alcohol. No illicit drugs. Worked in a refugee camp in Ethiopia.
stage2.physical_exam: Cachectic male, drowsy but arousable, oriented only to person. Mild nuchal rigidity. Positive Kernig sign. Cranial nerve exam: left lateral rectus palsy (CN VI) with horizontal diplopia on left gaze; right pupil slightly larger than left but both reactive. Fundoscopy: bilateral papilledema. No other cranial nerve deficits. Mild bilateral upper extremity hyperreflexia. No focal motor weakness. No skin rash, lymphadenopathy, or hepatosplenomegaly. Lungs clear bilaterally.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Elevated opening pressure consistent with TB meningitis; contributes to cranial neuropathies and papilledema
Lymphocytic pleocytosis is the hallmark of TB meningitis; early disease may have mixed or neutrophilic predominance
Markedly elevated protein from basilar meningeal inflammation and blood-brain barrier disruption
Profound hypoglycorrhachia (CSF/serum glucose ratio <0.3) is characteristic of TB meningitis; glucose consumed by mycobacteria and inflammatory cells

lab_results.result: Rare acid-fast bacilli seen on concentrated smear
lab_results.clinical_significance: Low sensitivity (10-20%) but highly specific when positive; concentrated large-volume specimens improve yield

lab_results.result: Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex detected; rifampin resistance not detected
lab_results.clinical_significance: Rapid molecular confirmation of TB meningitis with rifampin susceptibility — critical for timely initiation of appropriate therapy
ADA >10 U/L in CSF supports TB meningitis; >8 U/L has sensitivity ~80% and specificity ~90%
Peripheral WBC is often normal in TB meningitis
Hyponatremia from SIADH or cerebral salt wasting, common in TB meningitis (occurs in ~40-50% of cases)
HIV negative; TB meningitis occurs in both immunocompetent and immunocompromised patients
Indicates prior TB exposure/latent TB; supportive but does not distinguish latent from active disease

imaging_results.result: Diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement, most prominent in the basal cisterns (interpeduncular, suprasellar, and ambient cisterns). Communicating hydrocephalus with dilated lateral and third ventricles. Small tuberculoma (8 mm ring-enhancing lesion) in the right temporal lobe. No acute infarct on DWI.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Basal meningeal enhancement is the hallmark of TB meningitis; hydrocephalus from impaired CSF absorption; tuberculoma confirms CNS TB involvement

imaging_results.result: Normal lung fields. No hilar lymphadenopathy or miliary pattern. No prior granulomatous disease changes.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Up to 50% of patients with TB meningitis have normal chest X-rays; absence of pulmonary TB does not exclude TB meningitis

key_tests: CSF AFB smear and mycobacterial culture (gold standard, but takes 2-6 weeks)
CSF Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra (rapid PCR — sensitivity ~70-90% for TB meningitis)
acceptable_tests: Serum sodium (screen for SIADH/cerebral salt wasting)
CSF cryptococcal antigen (to exclude cryptococcal meningitis)
low_yield_tests: Serum TB antibody tests (serologic tests for TB are NOT recommended by WHO)
Lumbar spine MRI (unless clinical concern for spinal TB)

stage5.final_clue: An Ethiopian immigrant with subacute progressive headache, fever, confusion, CN VI palsy, and papilledema.

debrief.classic_presentation: Subacute meningitis (developing over 1-4 weeks, not hours) with progressive headache, fever, meningismus, and cranial neuropathies (CN VI most common, followed by CN III and VII) in a patient from a TB-endemic region. The CSF classic triad: lymphocytic pleocytosis (100-500 cells), markedly elevated protein (>100 mg/dL), and profoundly low glucose (CSF/serum ratio <0.3). Hyponatremia from SIADH and basilar meningeal enhancement on MRI are characteristic. The clinical stages are: Stage I (clear consciousness, no focal deficits), Stage II (confusion, focal deficits, cranial neuropathies), Stage III (coma or hemiplegia).
debrief.attending_mode_notes: TB meningitis has the highest mortality of all forms of TB (15-40% even with treatment), and neurologic sequelae are common in survivors. The Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra has significantly improved rapid diagnosis (sensitivity ~70-90% in CSF vs. ~50-60% for the original Xpert), but a negative result does NOT exclude TB meningitis — clinical judgment and empiric treatment are critical. Repeat LP (large volume, ≥6 mL) on consecutive days increases diagnostic yield. The CSF AFB smear sensitivity can be improved from ~10% to ~60% by examining centrifuged sediment from large-volume (≥6 mL) samples with prolonged reading time. Hydrocephalus is the most common complication and may require ventriculoperitoneal shunting or serial LPs. Adjunctive dexamethasone improves survival (Thwaites et al., NEJM 2004) and should be given to ALL patients with TB meningitis regardless of HIV status, for 6-8 weeks with taper. Isoniazid and pyrazinamide have the best CSF penetration among first-line TB drugs; rifampin and ethambutol have limited CNS penetration. Paradoxical worsening (IRIS) can occur 2-8 weeks after starting treatment, with new tuberculomas or worsening hydrocephalus — manage with continued TB treatment and corticosteroids, not a change in antibiotics.
debrief.pathophysiology: TB meningitis results from hematogenous dissemination of M. tuberculosis to the meninges, typically from a primary pulmonary or extrapulmonary focus. Rich and McCordock (1933) described the mechanism: a subependymal or subpial caseous focus (Rich focus) that forms during initial bacteremia ruptures into the subarachnoid space, triggering a granulomatous inflammatory response concentrated in the basal meninges (the thick, gelatinous basal exudate). This basal meningeal inflammation causes: (1) cranial nerve entrapment (CN VI is most vulnerable due to its long intracranial course), (2) obstruction of CSF flow through the basal cisterns leading to communicating hydrocephalus, (3) vasculitis of the arteries of the Circle of Willis with risk of ischemic stroke (particularly the middle cerebral artery territory), and (4) encephalitis from direct parenchymal invasion. The profound hypoglycorrhachia results from impaired glucose transport across the inflamed blood-brain barrier and consumption by mycobacteria and inflammatory cells.
debrief.treatment_overview: Initiate empiric anti-TB therapy IMMEDIATELY upon clinical suspicion — do NOT wait for culture confirmation. Standard regimen: intensive phase (2 months): isoniazid 5 mg/kg/day + rifampin 10 mg/kg/day + pyrazinamide 25 mg/kg/day + ethambutol 15 mg/kg/day. Continuation phase (7-10 months): isoniazid + rifampin. Total duration for TB meningitis: 9-12 months (longer than pulmonary TB). Adjunctive dexamethasone: 0.3-0.4 mg/kg/day IV for 4 weeks, then taper over 4 weeks (reduces mortality by ~30%). Pyridoxine 50 mg/day to prevent isoniazid-induced neuropathy. Monitor for hydrocephalus progression (may require EVD or VP shunt). Monitor hepatotoxicity (isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide — check LFTs at baseline, 2, 4, and 8 weeks). Correct hyponatremia with fluid restriction if SIADH confirmed. For this patient at Stage II: start all 4 drugs + dexamethasone immediately, repeat imaging in 2-4 weeks, and consider neurosurgical consultation for hydrocephalus management.
high_yield_pearls: TB meningitis is the most lethal form of TB — mortality is 15-40% even with treatment, and delays in starting empiric therapy worsen outcomes; treat on clinical suspicion, not lab confirmation.
The CSF triad of lymphocytic pleocytosis + very high protein (>100 mg/dL) + very low glucose (CSF/serum ratio <0.3) in a subacute meningitis should trigger immediate TB meningitis evaluation.
Up to 50% of patients with TB meningitis have a normal chest X-ray — absence of pulmonary disease does NOT exclude TB meningitis.
Adjunctive dexamethasone reduces mortality by ~30% in TB meningitis and should be given to ALL patients regardless of HIV status.

Basal meningeal enhancement on MRI is the radiologic hallmark of TB meningitis, distinguishing it from viral (typically no enhancement) and bacterial meningitis (diffuse rather than basal predominance).
sources: Thwaites GE et al. Dexamethasone for the treatment of tuberculous meningitis in adolescents and adults. N Engl J Med. 2004;351(17):1741-1751.

Nahid P et al. Official American Thoracic Society/Centers for Disease Control and Prevention/Infectious Diseases Society of America Clinical Practice Guidelines: Treatment of Drug-Susceptible Tuberculosis. Clin Infect Dis. 2016;63(7):e147-e195.
UpToDate: Central nervous system tuberculosis — An overview (2024)
Marais S et al. Tuberculous meningitis: a uniform case definition for use in clinical research. Lancet Infect Dis. 2010;10(11):803-812.

after_stage2_more_likely: TB meningitis — subacute meningitis (3 weeks), Ethiopian immigrant, CN VI palsy, papilledema, nuchal rigidity, and progressive encephalopathy; epidemiology and tempo strongly suggest TB
Cryptococcal meningitis — subacute course with elevated ICP can present similarly, but more typical in HIV-positive patients with very low CD4 counts
after_stage2_less_likely: Viral meningoencephalitis — typically more acute onset (days, not weeks) and would not explain cranial neuropathy with this degree of meningismus
Neurosarcoidosis — could cause basilar meningitis and CN palsies but would be unusual as a new diagnosis in this epidemiologic context
after_labs_top3: TB meningitis — Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra positive, AFB seen on smear, lymphocytic pleocytosis with profoundly low glucose (ratio 0.18), markedly elevated protein, elevated ADA, basal meningeal enhancement on MRI, hydrocephalus
Cryptococcal meningitis — can present similarly but CSF India ink and cryptococcal antigen would be positive (not tested here but HIV negative makes it less likely)
Partially treated bacterial meningitis — lymphocytic shift can occur after antibiotic exposure, but this patient had no prior antibiotics, and MTB PCR confirms the diagnosis
